data.title: JPMorgan Chase: The Profit Engine That Remains Undervalued
data.description:
Markets surged to fresh record highs on the back of July’s consumer inflation reading. The data increased expectations for a rate cut at the Federal Reserve’s September meeting. Trade sentiment also improved after President Trump extended the U.S.–China tariff truce for an additional 90 days.

While the tone in markets has been broadly optimistic, valuations remain elevated. The S&P 500 trades at valuation levels not seen since the dot-com crash.

Identifying stocks that combine strong fundamentals with cheap valuations should remain a priority for diligent investors. Our AI agent is designed to screen for such opportunities by leveraging proven-superior data to produce more precise, transparent, and reliable signals.

Our Long Idea this week highlights a company with industry-leading profitability, revenue growth across all business segments, record profits, and a cheap stock price.

We originally made JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) a Long Idea in May 2020 and most recently reiterated our position on the stock in November 2024.

JPMorgan Chase recently beat both top- and bottom-line estimates in 2Q25, and now that the company has filed its latest 10-Q, we find that the stock still presents Very Attractive risk/reward.

JPM offers favorable Risk/Reward based on the company’s:

  • Strong growth across both interest income and non-interest income segments,
  • Record high profits,
  • Industry-leading capital ratios and profitability leader, and
  • Cheap stock valuation.

What’s Working

JPMorgan Chase reported strong results across its business in 2Q25. The company’s firmwide average loans and deposits were up 5% and 6% year-over-year (YoY), respectively. More specifically, JPMorgan Chase reported quality growth across all three business segments, including:

  • Debit and credit card sales volume was up 7% YoY in the Consumer & Community banking segment.
  • Investment banking fees were up 7% YoY, and JPMorgan ranked #1 for Global Investment Banking fees with 8.9% wallet share year-to-date (YTD) in its Commercial & Investment Bank segment.
  • Assets under management increased 18% YoY to $4.3 trillion, including $80 billion in net inflows in its Asset & Wealth Management segment.

These results continue a trend of long-term fundamental strength for the largest bank in the United States.

Strong Business Operations and Fundamentals

JPMorgan Chase’s revenue composition reflects a strategically balanced model, with total net revenue split nearly evenly between net interest income [NII] and noninterest revenue [NIR]. This diversification mitigates earnings volatility tied to interest rate fluctuations and reinforces the firm’s resilience across market cycles.

Notably, JPMorgan Chase has demonstrated consistent growth in both revenue streams over the past decade. The company grew NII from $43.6 billion in 2014 to $93.2 billion in the TTM ended 2Q25, which represents compound annual growth of 7%. JPMorgan Chase grew its NIR from $50.6 billion to $82.4 billion over the same time, or 5% compounded annually. See Figure 1.

Figure 1: JPMorgan Chase’s NII and NIR: 2014 – TTM

JPMorgan Chase leverages its diversified business model to continually grow its top and bottom-line.

JPMorgan Chase has grown revenue and net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) by 10% and 11% compounded annually since 2014. See Figure 2. In each of the past three fiscal years, and the TTM ended 2Q25, the company’s NOPAT reached record highs.

The company improved its NOPAT margin from 21% in 2014 to 23% in the TTM, while invested capital turns rose from 0.4 to 0.7 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margins and invested capital turns drive the company’s return on invested capital [ROIC] from 8% in 2014 to 16% in the TTM ended 2Q25.

Additionally, the company’s Core Earnings, a superior and cleaner earnings measure, grew 10% compounded annually from $22.2 billion in 2014 to $60.6 billion in the TTM.

Figure 2: JPMorgan Chase’s Revenue and NOPAT Since 2014

Industry-Leading Profitability

JPMorgan Chase has not only grown revenue and profits to record levels, but it has done so while maintaining industry leading margins and ROIC. As we mentioned earlier in the report, the company has improved both its operational efficiency (NOPAT margin) and its balance sheet efficiency (invested capital turns) over the last decade.

Per Figure 3, JPMorgan Chase has the highest NOPAT margin and ROIC among peers, which include, Goldman Sachs (GS), Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), Bank of America (BAC), and Wells Fargo (WFC).

Figure 3: JPMorgan Chase’s Profitability vs. Peers: TTM

Safety That Lasts

Due to its superior capitalization, JPMorgan Chase maintains a stronger financial position than its peers, too.

At the end of 2Q25, JPMorgan Chase’s Common Equity Tier 1 Capital Ratio (CET1), the equity portion of its Tier 1 Capital, was 15.1%, which is up from 12.4% at the end of 2019 and 10.2% in 2014. Importantly, JPMorgan Chase’s CET1 ratio is well above the 4.5% minimum required by the Federal Reserve.

Additionally, the company’s Tier 1 Capital ratio, which measures a bank’s core equity capital to its total risk-weighted assets, is 16.1% through the 2Q25, which is up from 14.1% in 2019 and 11.6% in 2014. Once again, JPMorgan Chase’s Tier 1 Capital ratio is well above the 6.0% minimum required by the Federal Reserve.

JPMorgan Chase’s Tier 1 Capital and CET 1 ratios have been trending higher for over a decade. See Figure 4.

Figure 4: Tier 1 Capital and Common Equity Tier 1 Capital Ratios: 2010 – 2Q25

JPMorgan Chase leads the industry when it comes to capital ratios.

Per Figure 5, JPMorgan Chase’s CET1 ratio ranks above big bank peers mentioned earlier in the report. The company’s Tier 1 Capital ratio is higher than all listed peers except Morgan Stanley.

Figure 5: JPMorgan Chase’s Capital Ratios vs. Peers – 2Q25

Potential for 5%+ Yield

JPMorgan Chase has paid $87.7 billion (11% of market cap) in dividends since 2019 and increased its quarterly dividend from $0.80/share in 2Q19 to $1.40/share in 2Q25. The company’s current dividend, when annualized, provides a 1.9% yield.

JPMorgan Chase also returns capital to shareholders through share repurchases. Since 2019, JPMorgan Chase has repurchased $95.9 billion (12% of market cap) shares. During the first six months of 2025, the company repurchased 15.1 billion shares.

In July 2025, JPMorgan Chase’s Board of Directors authorized a repurchase program of $50 billion. Should the company repurchase shares at the TTM pace, it would repurchase $25.7 billion of shares over the next 12 months, which is 3.2% of the company’s current market cap.

When combined, the dividend and share repurchase yield could reach 5.1%.

Strong Cash Flows Support Shareholder Return

Investors should take comfort in knowing JPMorgan Chase will be able to afford to pay its dividends and repurchase shares due to its large free cash flow (FCF) generation. From 2019 through 1H25, JPMorgan Chase generated $201.2 billion in FCF, which equals 25% of the company’s enterprise value.

Figure 6: JPMorgan Chase’s Cumulative Free Cash Flow: 2019 – 1H25

JPMorgan Chase’s $201.2 billion in FCF since 2019 is more than enough to cover its $183.6 billion in combined dividend payments ($87.7 billion) and share repurchases ($95.9 billion).

JPMorgan Chase’s repurchases have also meaningfully reduced its shares outstanding from 3.1 billion in 2019 to 2.7 billion in 2Q25. See Figure 7.

We like companies that choose to return capital to shareholders instead of spending it on costly acquisitions or executive bonuses that rarely drive shareholder value creation. Companies that sport strong enough cash flows to consistently lower their shares outstanding offer excellent value.

Figure 7: JPMorgan Chase’s Shares Outstanding: 2019 – 2Q25

What’s Not Working

Potential Rate Cuts in the Remainder of 2025

Expectations for a rate cut in September are on the rise, in part due to the latest CPI data and comments from Fed officials. Fed Governor Bowman aims for three interest rate cuts in the remainder of 2025. An interest rate cut would put pressure on all ’banks’—JPMorgan Chase included—net interest margin (NIM) and, in turn, their NII.

In its 2Q25 10-Q, JPMorgan Chase disclosed its earnings-at-risk sensitivity depending on different movements in rates. In this analysis, JPMorgan Chase determined that a 100-basis point interest rate decline results in a $2 billion decrease in earnings. Similarly, a 200-basis point interest rate decline results in a nearly $5 billion decrease in earnings.

The good news? This exposure to lower rates is down from the company’s sensitivity in December 2024, which coincides with rising expectations for a rate cut.

JPMorgan Chase maintains the best capital ratios in the industry and is best positioned to grow noninterest revenue throughout all cycles.

Additionally, any pressure on profits is already more than priced into JPMorgan Chase’s current stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies Profits Never Grow Again

At its current price, JPMorgan Chase has a price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio of 1.0. This ratio means the market expects the company’s profits to never grow from TTM ended 2Q25 levels. For reference, JPMorgan Chase has grown NOPAT 11% compounded annually over the last five, ten, and 26 (longest period in our model) years.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to quantify the cash flow expectations for different stock price scenarios for JPM.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 20% (below the five-year average of 26% and TTM NOPAT margin of 23%) through 2034, and
  • Revenue grows 3% compounded annually through 2034 (compared to 13% CAGR over the last five years and 10% CAGR over the last decade), then

The stock would be worth $293/share today—nearly equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, JPMorgan Chase’s NOPAT would grow just 2% compounded annually from 2025–2034, which is well below the company’s historical NOPAT growth rates.

Shares Could Go 20%+ Higher Even If Profits Grow Well Below Historical Rates

If we instead assume:

  • NOPAT margin never improves and is maintained at 23% through 2034, and
  • Revenue grows 5% compounded annually through 2034 (compared to 13% CAGR over the last five years and 10% CAGR over the last decade), then

The stock would be worth $377/share today—a 20%+ upside to the current price. In this scenario, JPMorgan Chase’s NOPAT would grow 5% compounded annually through fiscal 2034, which would still be below the company’s NOPAT compound annual growth rates over the past 5-, 10-, 15-, and 20-year periods.

Should NOPAT grow closer to historical rates, the upside would be even larger.

Figure 8 compares JPMorgan Chase’s historical NOPAT to the NOPAT implied in each of the above scenarios.

Figure 8: JPMorgan Chase’s Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

Disclosure: David Trainer owns JPM.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the newest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street on Friday posted its best five-day advance in over a month, after a host of inflation data further reinforced expectations of a Federal Reserve interest rate cut next week.

On Wednesday, the August producer price index (PPI) unexpectedly slipped on an M/M basis for both core and headline measures. The readings also decelerated on a Y/Y basis from July. Then, on Thursday, the headline August consumer price index (CPI) came in a tad hotter than expected, while the core CPI matched July's rise.

Following recent evidence of cooling in the labor market, the PPI and CPI reports further baked in the odds of interest rate cuts by the Fed this year. data.title: ULTY: 100%+ Yield That Has To Be Ignored
data.description:
Option-driven strategies have lately gained significant traction in the market. Arguably, the most popular type of instrument is a covered call ETF. The options sold allow for the generation of premiums, which, in turn, can be directly distributed out to investors, thereby pushing up the yield in a quite notable fashion.

It is also worth mentioning that the architects and managers of these types of instruments are usually well-established brand names in the financial industry. For example, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) is managed by JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) and Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) by Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS). Obviously, JEPI and GPIQ are not their only covered call ETF products. Then we also have a some specialized "boutique" firms, which focus specifically on ETF construction.

My favorite is the Neos Funds, which has optimized their covered call ETFs for tax efficiency and dynamic hedging processes in order to further mitigate the potential downside risks. Think here of the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), which has received the best new active ETF award this year. I have also circulated a bullish piece on it - here.

Speaking of specialized ETF firms, I have to mention YieldMax, which has a plethora of extremely high-yielding ETFs.

One of them is YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY), which is a great example to illustrate why prudent investors should, in my humble opinion, stay away from these kinds of vehicles.

Thesis

Let's start with the basics.

ULTY’s primary investment objective is to deliver high current income. The secondary objective is linked to capturing some price appreciation. This is standard for not only covered call ETFs, but also many closed-end funds such as the PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF).

ULTY is actively managed, just like one of my favorite covered call ETFs - QQQI. Yet, the net expense ratio here is 1.3%, which is significantly higher than the average in the sector (it varies from 0.35% to 0.75%). Let's also remember that 1.3% includes a 0.1% temporary fee waiver, which is set to expire by the end of February, 2026.

Theoretically, part of this could be offset by a strategic advantage that is offered by ULTY - i.e., weekly payout mechanism. Each week, the management has to optimize and calibrate the option premium inflows to accommodate such frequent payouts. For income-oriented investors, this feature should definitely sound attractive (especially, for those who live off current income and want to synchronize living expenses with incoming cash flows).

Yet, the key driver behind the higher cost base is the underlying complexity of the ETF. ULTY is not a traditional covered call ETF, which a) goes long a particular underlying such as the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) or the S&P 500 (SPY) and b) sells options against these long positions.

Instead, ULTY typically selects between 15 and 30 securities and then opens up positions in those in a synthetic manner by using specific option strategies. After this is completed, the ETF takes the next step by writing options against the synthetically created positions, leading to a synthetically covered call exposure.

I think that for investors it is worth digesting a bit how the synthetical long exposure works:

To achieve a synthetic long exposure to an Underlying Security, the Fund will buy that Underlying Security’s call options and, simultaneously, sell that Underlying Security’s put options to try to replicate the price movements of owning that Underlying Security. The call options purchased by the Fund and the put options sold by the Fund will generally have one month to six-month terms and strike prices that are approximately equal to the then-current share price of the relevant Underlying Security at the time the options contracts are purchased and sold, respectively. The combination of the long call options and sold put options provides the Fund with investment exposure equal to approximately 100% of the relevant Underlying Securities for the duration of the applicable options exposure.

The key risk here, besides such technical dangers as pin risk, long call's time value decay (theta), option bid-ask spread widening, and early exercise of the short put, is that ULTY is subject to all potential losses if the underlying shares decrease in greater value than the collected income streams and do so in a rapid and deep manner. In other words, it would take, say, 10% market shock and activated circuit breakers to most probably destroy most of ULTY's NAV. Given that ULTY cherry picks securities based on implied vol levels (i.e., the higher the vol, the more likely it will be included in the mix), I would not rule out such a probability. For example, ULTY holds Carvana Co. (CVNA), Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) and Palantir Technologies (PLTR) - stocks that on any given day could very likely register a sharp correction to the downside.

Having said that, some of you might think that risks are high indeed, but certainly justified given the yield of ~120%. Unfortunately, this ~120% is not pure income-driven yield, and instead is heavily funded by the underlying NAV.

Let me illustrate this through two charts.

Here we can see how significant ULTY's NAV erosion has been since its inception, and that, on a total return basis, the result is suboptimal. Meanwhile, QQQI, which is a covered call ETF with direct underlying exposure to the securities, has managed to both deliver attractive income streams and some decent price appreciation as well.

And here we can see the consequence of NAV decay and how they have fed into declining dividends.

The bottom line

In a nutshell, I think that ULTY is an excellent example of how value traps work. They offer optically enticing yields, which sooner or later will get cut, leading to a subpar total return performance (even if in the initial periods the high coupons are collected and included in the return calculus).

The fact that ULTY is just one large correction away from registering another huge income cut and also considering the inherent NAV-decay mechanism, it is something that I would not recommend for prudent income investors.

My suggestion would be to take a closer look at safer covered call ETFs, which are not based on synthetically created positions. The good thing is that the yields investors can obtain from these safer ETFs are still enticing - close to 10% for GPIQ and almost 14% for QQQI.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings: Missing A Starting Industry Rebound
data.description:
Hooker Furnishings Corporation (NASDAQ:HOFT) reported the company’s fiscal Q2 results from the May-July period on the 11th of September. The furnishings company reported concerning sales in the Home Meridian segment, causing market concerns over the company’s performance as industry sales are already rebounding. Aggressive cost savings are only offsetting severe sales weakness.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous February 2024 article, titled “Hooker Furnishings: Temporary Headwinds Haven't Created An Attractive Buying Level”. The stock has since lost a further -55% of its value, significantly underperforming the S&P 500’s 29% gain.

Hooker Furnishings’ Weakness Continues with Home Meridian

Hooker Furnishings continued to report declining sales in the latest fiscal Q2 report. Revenues came in at $82.1 million, down by -13.6% year-on-year. The quarter followed Hooker Furnishings’ mostly consistent sales declines from late 2021 forward as housing sales have remained weak and the consumer sentiment is poor. On the surface, sales weakness only seems to be continuing a longer trend.

Yet, as the market’s post-earnings reaction suggests, continued sales weakness is now cause for high concern - the topline result missed Wall Street’s consensus by $9.0 million, as most of the year-on-year revenue decline came as a surprise. Industry-wide furnishings sales have already picked up in recent months, latest still showing 5.1% year-on-year growth in July, according to FRED data; the surprisingly weak results were Hooker Furnishings’ own doing this time around, as the industry is already cautiously rebounding. As comparisons, Ethan Allen (ETD) most recently reported a much smaller -4.9% revenue decline, Arhaus returned to strong 15.7% revenue growth, Bassett Furniture (BSET) reported 1.1% growth, Flexsteel (FLXS) reported 3.4% growth, and Lovesac (LOVE) reported 2.5% growth. Hooker Furnishings is missing the rebound with apparent market share losses.

Hooker Furnishings relates the weakness to a $13.6 million sales decline in the Home Meridian segment, while other segments remained stable in a year-on-year comparison. Home Meridian’s project-based hospitality business previously had high shipments in Q2’FY25 and one of the segment’s major customers filed for bankruptcy last year, causing the majority of Home Meridian’s weakness. On top, general macroeconomic unrest seemed to weigh significantly on the segment. Most concerningly, Home Meridian’s weakness doesn’t seem to be a one-off hiccup as the segment’s incoming orders continued to decline across customers. The loss of one large customer and lower project-based revenues are more transitory in nature, but declines in traditional channels underline more persistent concerns. It seems that Hooker Furnishings’ market share losses will continue, combining with a still uncertain industry backdrop.

Weaker sales caused Hooker Furnishings’ profitability to decline further. The operating loss widened by $1.3 million to $4.4 million despite aggressive Hooker Furnishings’ cost savings, and the Q2 EPS of -$0.31 missed Wall Street’s consensus by $0.19 – Home Meridian’s weakness is causing all-around weakness for the company at an unexpected scale.

The outlook doesn’t seem to be getting much better; Home Meridian’s orders are still on a decline, and other segments’ better backlog developments don’t seem to be enough to support revenues in upcoming quarters. Hooker Furnishings is going through severe restructuring to achieve $25 million in cost savings across the organization by FY2027, but cost reductions are barely able to hold earnings above board – Hooker Furnishings’ past twelve months’ operating loss now stands at -$15.1 million. Home Meridian’s sales deleverage is driving the earnings narrative.

Ultimately, the weak report does require investors to take a longer-term view. Hooker Furnishings has minimal cash but $57.7 million in borrowing capacity, providing necessary liquidity to navigate further uncertainty. The furnishings industry still has massive rebound potential with normalizing housing sales – the current negative earnings don’t reflect Hooker Furnishings’ performance in a normal market. For now, as the housing market is still navigating high mortgage rates and other uncertainties, a further rebound stands on an uncertain timeline.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Hooker Furnishings. I now assume sales weakness to continue through FY2026, but that sales will rebound afterwards in the FY2027-FY2029 period through an industry recovery.

As I estimate much lower revenues than previously even in normal market conditions, I expect Hooker Furnishings’ profitability to remain low despite cost savings. I estimate the EBIT margin to only stabilize at 3.5% in a base scenario. Hooker Furnishings should now convert after-tax earnings well into free cash flow as the company has moderated capital expenditures significantly.

The estimates put Hooker Furnishings’ fair value at $9.24, fairly near the stock price at the time of writing. The stock’s sell-off seems to have been justified as the investment now comes with increased long-term earnings uncertainty. Due to a volatile short- to long-term earnings outlook, the DCF model’s fair value is also highly volatile – Hooker Furnishings could continue to decline or could rebound strongly if the company manages to stabilize earnings, and the current valuation reflects quite a balanced risk-to-reward.

The fair value estimate is down from $24.72 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.79%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Hooker Furnishings to use a notable amount of debt over the long term. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.01% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Seeking Alpha now estimates Hooker Furnishings’ beta at 1.36. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.79%.

Takeaway

Hooker Furnishings reported weak Q2 results due to the Home Meridian segment, and the segment’s weak orders suggest such pressure to continue despite the furnishings industry already showing a cautious rebound. Aggressive fixed cost savings are currently only enough to mostly offset sales deleverage. The investment remains highly volatile while the industry waits for a stronger rebound. As HOFT stock’s valuation provides a fairly balanced risk-to-reward, I remain with a Hold rating.

data.title: The GLP-1 Oral Frontier: Novo Nordisk And Eli Lilly's 100-Year Duel Enters Its Next Chapter
data.description:
Behind the Latest Moves

As of writing this analysis (2 Sep), I notice on the screen that in pre-market trading, Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) stock is up 1.5%, while Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) is similarly down 1.5%. While this has become fairly common lately - rivals' share prices have opposing movements - it's not always easy to interpret the exact reason behind the price actions similar to today's.

This time it wasn't too complicated - morning headlines revealed the results of another study. The study showed that Novo Nordisk's injectable weight-loss drug Wegovy (semaglutide) demonstrated 57% greater efficacy in reducing the risk of heart attack compared to rival Eli Lilly's tirzepatide, the active ingredient in both Mounjaro and Zepbound.

On August 26, almost the same thing happened, but the price action was more volatile - Eli Lilly rose 5%, while Novo Nordisk slipped around 2%. What puzzled me was that the headline driving the stock movement seemed to tell an entirely different story: Lilly's late-stage oral anti-obesity candidate, orforglipron, showed just 10.5% efficacy in the latest trial results. While that's a nice figure, it's lower than the 12.4% reported just a couple of weeks earlier- a headline that triggered a 14% drop in Lilly's stock. The weaker efficacy number has to be a meaningful miss, yet Lilly's stock rises by 5%!

The devil was in the details, as they say, and often happens to be—while the released trial numbers for orforglipron weren't groundbreaking in absolute terms, they were relatively strong compared to Novo's similar competing late-stage candidates.

What’s also important is to differentiate: is the headline discussing weight-loss effectiveness in the context of anti-obesity treatments, or is it referring to the same drug’s effectiveness when used for diabetes-related weight loss? Eli Lilly itself offers clarification to help better understand the positioning of their drug candidate as follows:

Orforglipron is an investigational, once-daily oral GLP-1 medication being studied for the treatment of type 2 diabetes and obesity. It belongs to a class of medicines called GLP-1 receptor agonists and is designed to help lower blood sugar and support weight loss. It comes in a pill form. In clinical trials, it was taken once a day at any time, without food or water restrictions.

All in all, a brief explanation for such share price movements could be the following: orforglipron was anticipated to surpass Novo's Rybelsus—the current market leader and the only GLP-1 pill available for oral diabetes treatment at the moment. The surprise came from the superior trial results compared to Novo's PIONEER PLUS data, which featured their late-stage diabetes candidates, 25 mg and 50 mg semaglutide.

In addition, Eli Lilly's bold move to seek early FDA clearance made the case even more compelling. Although orforglipron’s data for diabetes is only marginally superior—by about one percentage point of effectiveness—a difference some might consider statistically insignificant with no real-world advantage—Lilly's attempt to gain approval sooner than Novo could secure a crucial first-mover advantage, an important factor given that its rival has roughly similar data points.

While Lilly has publicly announced it will seek FDA submission for orforglipron for diabetes this year, Novo Nordisk's next step with their candidates in the sector remains unknown. They filed for approval of 25 mg semaglutide for anti-obesity without diabetes earlier this year and are likely waiting for the FDA's decision for this indication first before deciding their next step regarding diabetes management. The FDA decision is expected to be announced soon, possibly within the next few months. Before their latest move, Eli Lilly was widely expected to submit anti-obesity approval first, followed by diabetes.

Eli Lilly may have outmaneuvered Novo Nordisk to some extent in the diabetes market and, based on recent developments, could potentially gain market access by the end of 2026. Still, Novo Nordisk's obesity treatment, oral Wegovy, could gain access as early as late this year or early next. If Novo submits a diabetes application also in the coming months, they could potentially launch their diabetes version of semaglutide around the same time as Lilly.

The 100-Year Rivalry and the Rapid Progress of the Anti-Obesity Sector

Insulin

The rivalry between Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk dates back more than 100 years. Both companies entered the diabetes market in 1923 when Lilly launched the world's first commercially available insulin product, Iletin. That same year, Novo's predecessor, Nordisk Insulinlaboratorium, began commercializing its insulin production after the underlying technique was brought to Denmark from Canada by August Krogh.

As NobelPrize.org highlights, the discovery of insulin in 1922 transformed diabetes from a death sentence into a manageable condition, dramatically improving life expectancy.

GLP-1

Eli Lilly was an early pioneer in GLP-1-type treatments through its partnership with Amylin Pharmaceuticals. The collaboration led to the launch of a drug called Byetta in 2005—the first GLP-1 receptor agonist approved for type 2 diabetes. Despite its groundbreaking status, Byetta never became a blockbuster, largely due to its twice-daily injection schedule. Still, it offered meaningful benefits, including improved glycemic control and modest weight loss, laying the foundation for the GLP-1's incredible success that followed.

The silent revolution of focusing on treating obesity without diabetes, as we know it today, actually began with the popularity of Ozempic for off-label use. Ozempic was approved by the FDA at the end of 2017 and was brought to market soon after the decision. It became a blockbuster already in 2019, generating roughly $1 billion that year and reaching $2.6 billion by 2020. Ozempic could be rewarded as the flagship of the GLP-1 market's commercial success.

When Novo Nordisk noticed the significant off-label demand, it started to develop a drug specifically meant for anti-obesity - Wegovy. In the real world, Wegovy is just a more potent formulation of Ozempic—they both contain the same active ingredient, semaglutide, but Wegovy has a 2.4-times higher dose consisting of 2.4 mg semaglutide compared to Ozempic's 1 mg.

The launch of Wegovy in June 2021 was once again a massive success, but the company immediately faced a major hurdle: demand, combined with Ozempic sales, outstripped supply, leading to critical shortages. In its first full year of sales in 2022, the drug generated just a bit under $1 billion in revenue. This overwhelming success led to semaglutide injection products being added to the Food and Drug Administration’s [FDA] drug shortage list in March 2022 for Wegovy and August 2022 for Ozempic.

It was the perfect moment for old rival Lilly to enter the market with their novelty Mounjaro, whose development had gained momentum back in 2018—right when Novo’s success with Ozempic was getting widespread attention. Mounjaro was launched in May 2022 and quickly gained traction, partly supported by Novo's struggles. Lilly followed up with Zepbound in 2023, which was actually just the same tirzepatide, a re-labeled version of Mounjaro approved specifically for obesity.

In 2022, Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly collectively held around 50% of the diabetes and obesity market, with Novo's share at roughly 35% compared to Lilly's 15%. However, by 2024, the explosive growth of the GLP-1 market has enabled Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly to collectively hold nearly three-quarters of the entire diabetes and obesity drug sector—including insulin and other older diabetes medications. In the first half of 2025, Eli Lilly's rapid growth enabled it to overtake its century-old competitor and take the leadership position in its home field -the U.S. market.

While Lilly has gained momentum in the U.S., Novo still holds a strong lead in international markets outside the U.S.

To add even more color to the rivalry, the "SHAPE" study, sponsored by Eli Lilly and published in May 2025 in the New England Journal of Medicine, found that tirzepatide (the original compound behind Mounjaro and Zepbound) users lost about 20.2% of their body weight, whereas those on semaglutide lost just about 13.7% over 72 weeks. However, at its R&D Investor event in June 2025, Novo Nordisk presented the same survey data but showed a different outcome.

While the differences in reported results may stem from the nature of the data and methodology, the outcome shows tirzepatide superiority over semaglutide.

Better real-world metrics are the key driver behind Eli Lilly's catch-up, gaining momentum, bringing the GLP-1 sector globally closer to an even split between old rivals. As of the first half of 2025, Novo Nordisk still continues to lead the global GLP-1 market. The company reported half-year-related sales revenue of $18.1 billion, accounting for slightly more than half of total global GLP-1 sales. Eli Lilly sold the same type of drugs worth $17 billion during the period.

While the battle for the leaderboard in the obesity market is moving at an incredible pace, the landscape is about to change. Oral anti-obesity drugs have the potential to shake up the 100-year rivalry yet again.

How the Rivals Compare in Value

Novo Nordisk's financial ratios are quite comparable to those of other major pharmaceutical companies, yet its strong growth trajectory indicates that it may be undervalued. While Eli Lilly's metrics are clearly above the peer average, its growth numbers are also significantly better. Given the projected growth prospects of the GLP-1 sector, I believe Eli Lilly's relatively higher valuation is justified.

Additionally, as both Novo and Lilly are high-growth stocks, I compare their forward P/E ratios with those of their peers projected for 2030.

Company Name P/E 2030 Dif %
Eli Lilly 14 38%
Novo Nordisk 9.2 -9%
AbbVie 11.4 12%
Roche 9.5 -6%
AstraZeneca 10 -2%
Novartis 10.4 2%
Merck 9.4 -7%
Amgen 11.2 10%
Pfizer 10 -2%
Sanofi 6.5 -36%
Sector Average 10.16

Source: Prepared by the author using Seeking Alpha data.

Based on the table, Novo Nordisk appears to be undervalued by 9%, while Eli Lilly seems to be overvalued by 38% when comparing their 2030 P/E ratios to sector peers. To gain additional individual insight, I compared Eli Lilly metrics with comparable sector medians and Lilly's own historical data:

We see that Lilly is currently relatively overvalued on both the trailing and one-year forward basis but undervalued compared to the sector median PEG Non-GAAP forward and their own five-year average forward-looking data.

While most data points indicate significant overvaluation, some present a more balanced perspective. In my view, Eli Lilly's price range of $700-750 feels reasonable considering growth ratios, but further gains seem unjustified until there's more clarity on pipeline developments and US drug price reforms. For now, I’d rate Eli Lilly as a Hold.

I believe Novo Nordisk's greater dependence on non-U.S. markets makes it less vulnerable to U.S. regulatory reforms. While its fundamental valuation is similar to or slightly lower than its peers, its superior growth prospects could justify a higher price target. Compared to its peers and factoring in higher growth rates, it appears to be at least 10% undervalued. However, when looking at historical valuation metrics, it could potentially be undervalued by as much as 50%.

In conclusion, considering the risks from US drug price reforms and the market's likely overly conservative view of its growth potential, I believe Novo's fair price target is 15-20% higher than its current level, falling within the $65-70 range. I rate Novo shares as a Buy.

Overall, I think the oral marketplace opportunity might be broadly underestimated, and both stock valuations could be reassessed as prospects become clearer and oral drugs enter the market.

The Oral GLP-1 Perspective and Background

The FDA's approval of Novo Nordisk's Wegovy on June 4, 2021, for the treatment of obesity without diabetes symbolically marked the beginning of a new era. The surge in public awareness and wide use of weight-loss medications has created a bonanza in the pharma sector, making GLP-1 a shorthand for this massive opportunity among investors.

However, the most preferred and easiest-to-use drug form—the oral weight-loss pill with metrics nearly comparable to injectable leaders like Zepbound and Mounjaro—has not yet hit the market. While there are some old-school pills and the one newer GLP-1 oral diabetes drug, Rybelsus, launched in 2019 and developed and marketed by Novo Nordisk, they are nowhere near as effective as leading injectable options like Novo's Wegovy and Eli Lilly's Zepbound.

The idea of an easy-to-take tablet for weight loss is already over 60 years old. The pioneering drug called phentermine, introduced as early as 1959, was created to treat obesity in non-diabetic patients. While the idea was brilliant and the science was groundbreaking, the pills' use has always been subject to restrictions due to their stimulant effects.

Despite this, phentermine remains on the market even today, known by dozens of brand names, including Adipex, Ionamin, and Suprenza, with around 3 million prescriptions annually written in the U.S. alone. To put this in context, the US best-selling diabetes drug, Lilly's Mounjaro, receives roughly the same number of prescriptions in a quarter. The current oral diabetes leader, Rybelsus, receives fewer prescriptions than phentermine—around 1.5-2 million annually in the U.S.

However, phentermine has an entirely different mechanism of action from today's GLP-1 drugs. It is a sympathomimetic amine that acts as a central nervous system stimulant to suppress appetite. Due to its structural similarity to amphetamine, it is only approved for short-term use (up to 12 weeks) because of the risks of dependence and addiction.

Despite these limitations, phentermine has a notable effect on weight loss, considering it was created in the 1950s. Studies indicate that patients may lose an average of 3% of their initial body weight after a three-month course of treatment and between 5 and 7% after six months. This level of effectiveness places it in roughly the same range as Rybelsus, the current oral diabetes segment leader.

Phentermine may be regarded as a symbolic pioneer for the emerging generation of potent oral therapies targeting diabetes and obesity. Just as Wegovy proudly introduced a new chapter for weight-loss injectables, a powerful, once-daily oral pill with comparable metrics could once again change the sector's dynamic landscape. Its arrival is hotly anticipated, but it is still on its way.

The Importance of Oral Administration

The enduring and surprisingly large prescription count of phentermine provides an argument that patients are willing to use an oral drug, even if its effectiveness is relatively low. This patient preference is further validated by numerous surveys, which consistently show that people prefer taking a pill over getting an injection.

One example is the Revise survey conducted by Eli Lilly, highlighting people's preferences in choosing between injectables and oral pills. The question to participants was:

“Imagine you had the choice between two medications for your type 2 diabetes. One is a non‐insulin injectable medication that you would self‐inject under your skin once a week and the other is a pill you take by mouth every day. Both choices are similar in terms of the overall effectiveness, side effects, and cost. Which one would you choose?

The following table illustrates the outcome of the survey:

Currently, the only oral drug from the modern GLP-1 era on the market is Novo Nordisk's Rybelsus (semaglutide 14mg), marketed since 2019, which is intended for diabetes treatment. With no true competitors at the same level, it generates around $3.5 billion in annual sales. Its annual weight-loss effectiveness is in the ballpark of 4-5%, a significant difference compared to the ~20% weight loss achieved by injectable leaders like Zepbound and Mounjaro.

The Next Chapter: The Oral GLP-1 Competition

The oral anti-obesity market is poised at the starting line, ready to race toward very high rewards yet again in the GLP-1 space. While the overall anti-obesity treatment market is projected to grow by around 20%, there is a wide consensus that oral treatments will become the growth leaders once they hit the market. While projections may differ, the consensus is at least 30-40% CAGR until 2030.

Eli Lilly projects the total addressable GLP-1 market for obesity in the U.S. to be over 170 million patients. In its June 2025 ADA presentation, the company described the market and patient needs as follows:

As their presentation shows, Eli Lilly also highlights the importance of the route of administration, along with other key factors, when describing the segments of the GLP-1 market.

I am very optimistic about the demand for oral pills, as their ease of use is a crucial factor for gaining an even larger user base and therefore making the marketplace even larger as well. There are still many potential users for whom injecting themselves is a non-starter. Furthermore, it's not just about the injection itself but also the entire process, which is not a walk in the park. The medication needs to be kept in cold storage, and one has to find an appropriate time and place to administer it. In summary, there are plenty of reasons to believe that oral anti-obesity pills will meaningfully reshape the current marketplace dynamics.

While Eli Lilly’s current growth momentum with injectable Zepbound and Mounjaro is superior to Novo’s, having just taken the leadership position in the U.S., their position is in danger of changing once Novo Nordisk’s oral Wegovy is potentially approved later this year.

The data available as of writing this analysis, in my view, suggests that oral Wegovy will have a better start, benefiting from its first-mover advantage and superior effectiveness metrics on the anti-obesity front. However, according to Eli Lilly's latest data, released on August 26, the company is now in a stronger position with orforglipron in the diabetes market. The latest data reveals metrics that outperform Novo Nordisk's currently marketed Rybelsus effectiveness by about 2x, as well as their new oral diabetes candidates—semaglutide 25mg and 50mg—based on the PIONEER PLUS trial results.

Eli Lilly's orforglipron has superior metrics in managing diabetes and offers the flexibility of being taken at any time, regardless of meal cycles. In contrast, Novo's drug must be taken only on an empty stomach with a 30-minute wait before eating. While the ease of use might balance out its lower effectiveness in addressing obesity, current data suggest Lilly has the chance to dominate the diabetes space, while oral Wegovy holds an edge in the anti-obesity market.

Eli Lilly Oral Obesity Roadmap

Eli Lilly's latest presentation on orforglipron and the broader prospects of oral drug development:

The forward-looking next-generation pipeline innovations:

We can summarize here that while orforglipron has great potential considering the non-competitive marketplace currently, Eli Lilly's oral pipeline lacks late-stage contenders. Latest news on that front indicates that their leading candidate, naperiglipron, also has setbacks, and two Phase II trials out of three are terminated. Their oral strategy seems to focus on Orforglipron right now, which is expected to hit markets in late 2026 or early 2027.

Novo Nordisk's Oral Push

Novo Nordisk's oral Wegovy (semaglutide 25mg) is projected to enter the anti-obesity market in 2026, pending clearance by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration [FDA] and the European Medicines Agency [EMA]. Both applications are expected to be approved by late 2025 or early 2026.

The rest of the company's oral pipeline includes two other key candidates

  • Oral Wegovy (semaglutide 50mg): This drug has completed Phase III trials and is awaiting the next step. It is twice the dose of the very same 25mg drug submitted for approval. It has the potential to become the leading candidate for oral diabetes treatment.

  • Oral Amycretin: to begin Phase III trials.

Additionally, both companies have early-development programs for a new class of treatments aimed at taking anti-obesity treatments to a new level—called tri-agonists. Currently under development as injectable-only versions, these could potentially be progressed into oral forms in the future. However, they are still approximately 5-7 years away from reaching late-stage clinical trials if pursued.

Other Oral Drug Challengers

Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) has been seen as a potential disruptor to the existing duopoly, but its oral drug, VK2735, suffered a major setback just a couple of weeks ago. After Phase II data showed worse-than-expected tolerability, the company's share price plummeted by 41% on the day the data was released.

However, once the dust has settled, investors have taken a closer look at their underlying prospects. I have also written the analyses here. Compared to Lilly's and Novo's comparable figures, the belief is that the drug's chances still remain relatively strong, while they might not be as bright as previously thought. The main drawback of the trial data was too high a rate of participant discontinuations due to adverse effects. This is still seen as an area that can be potentially reworked moving forward with an improved trial design.

In addition, Viking has a solid balance sheet with $800 million in liquidity, which allows it three to four years to reevaluate the best path forward with its pipeline.

The next contender, Structure Therapeutics (GPCR), has achieved great advancements with its oral candidate, GSBR-1290:

Considering the latest announcements from Eli Lilly and Viking Therapeutics, the chances for Structure Therapeutics' oral drug might look relatively better than before. However, the company relies heavily on the results of its ongoing Phase II trial to gauge its chances. A key advantage of Structure is its healthy balance sheet, with over $700 million in cash on hand, which gives management plenty of room to consider its next steps.

Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) also has an oral program in addition to its injectables, called CT-996. In July 2024, the company released the very latest trial data showing that CT-996 led to a 7.3% reduction in body weight at a higher dose range but also caused a significant number of adverse events. Notably, nausea affected nearly 85% of participants at higher doses, in addition to other adverse issues. While the company expressed optimism and planned to initiate PHASE II trials in 2025, there hasn't been any further announcement so far. Considering their large cash coffers, they certainly don't need to rush and could weigh carefully if their candidate has enough potential for development.

The third potential competitor in the oral race is Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN), having their oral GLP-1 candidate, TERN-601, which is currently undergoing Phase 2 clinical trials.

The company's Phase 1 results, presented in September 2024, showed the drug was well-tolerated, with most side effects being mild to moderate. It also demonstrated modestly promising weight loss, with patients losing an average of almost 5% of their body weight over 28 days. The Phase 2 study and results are expected to be announced in early Q4 2025. However, while having $300 in cash and short-term investments, the latest news says that Terns will seek a partner for the TERN-601 development program. That could mean their prospects are weak to go forward with an oral GLP-1 candidate.

In summary, my take is that, while the space is very attractive, the potential competition in oral treatment is quite muted. The most interesting contenders are Novo oral Amycretin and Viking VK2735 oral version. However, Structure Therapeutics GSBR-1290 is a dark horse that could surprise, as it hasn't received much attention and therefore less coverage from analytics as well. The Lilly late-stage oral pipeline is empty, but Orforglipron, while Novo has promising Amycretin and ready-for-submission higher-dose semaglutide 50 mg in their arsenal. The Roche and Terns Pharmaceuticals candidates don't look highly promising as of now.

Risks

One of the biggest challenges pharmaceutical companies are currently facing is the administration's effort to lower drug prices in the U.S. The result of this ambitious effort is unpredictable, but as markets are already pricing in that risk, companies across the medical sector are feeling pressure, regardless of their individual fundamentals.

From a competition perspective, I believe the strongest contenders might be the Boehringer Ingelheim/Zealand Pharma collaboration, Viking Therapeutics, and Amgen MariTide. Amgen's and Boehringer's GLP-1 drugs are injectable only, lessening somewhat their prospects, while oral versions are highly likely to play an important role once Novo and Lilly launch their products.

Viking could emerge as a stronger competitor, having both injectable and oral formulations in late stages, the latter of which function as a dual agonist and have demonstrated impressive efficacy. However, the latest oral data somewhat reduced their chances, as tolerability appeared to be a more serious issue. In biotech, as we know, anything can happen before the FDA grants marketing approval.

Considering the GLP-1 competition more broadly, the risks are somewhat reduced as the sector is projected to grow fast - by the time any new player gets FDA or EMA approval, it's expected to be roughly twice as big as it is currently. Even if a new entrant succeeds and takes 5-10% of the market, it wouldn't be too bad for Novo and Lilly, given the size of the market by that time. Taking any market share won't be an easy task; the old rivals will undoubtedly do whatever they can to defend their position, and their strong lead gives them a significant edge to do so.

Beyond these, Lilly and Novo are facing the usual execution risks common to pharmaceutical companies, such as maintaining pricing power amid rising scrutiny from payers and governments, scaling manufacturing to meet potentially surging demand, etc. In addition, they must deliver on pipeline innovation to offset future patent cliffs and remain at the lead to maintain a long-term growth trajectory.

Takeaway

The century-long rivalry between these giants is moving into a new chapter, with contenders innovating and executing quickly to either take or keep the leadership position. While Eli Lilly's momentum is currently stronger, the oral drug rivalry is just accelerating and could reshape the landscape once again.

While Eli Lilly has made a compelling move by seeking an earlier-than-expected submission for its oral diabetes candidate, I believe Novo Nordisk's prospects broadly seem slightly better in the oral pill race. It's backed by a promising late-stage asset, oral Amycretin, along with a first-mover advantage in the anti-obesity market through oral Wegovy semaglutide 25 mg, as well as the regulatory readiness of its 50 mg oral semaglutide dose.

However, in the long run, the oral sector is still very much in the early stages of development and remains open to further innovation.

The fierce competition in the GLP-1 space is likely to have played a part in the sector's incredible success, innovating and broadening both the anti-obesity and diabetes markets, which in turn offers better lives to patients and greater returns to shareholders.

I rate Novo Nordisk a Buy due to its strong fundamentals and relatively lower dependence on U.S. drug price reform. My rating for Eli Lilly is a Hold. While its growth projections are currently impressive, its greater dependence on the U.S. market and administrative moves make it more sensitive to potential government efforts, especially since around half of its portfolio lies outside GLP-1 drugs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: BDCs Busy Bringing Bonds To Market
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events, as well as the top-down, providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the first week of September.

Market Action

BDCs were down slightly, outperforming only MLPs and Utilities on the week. PSEC reversed its previous outperformance and led to the downside. August marked the fourth positive month in a row for the sector after two down months in March and April.

The average valuation in our coverage is just shy of its long-term average.

Market Themes

Ares Capital (ARCC) is issuing a 5.1% bond due 2031. The company has been busy selling bonds. It issued a 5.5% bond in May and a 5.8% bond in January. Tightening in spreads has helped drive the coupons of its bond issuance lower this year, keeping interest expense contained. That said, the bonds that are coming due, like the 3.25% bond that matured in July and the 3.875% bond maturing in January, have significantly lower coupons than recent issuances, and this dynamic has continued to put upward pressure on BDC interest expense.

The drop in short-term rates has kept a lid on overall interest expense, as most BDCs also have sizable floating-rate credit facilities. However, that has not prevented overall net investment income from falling, given about half of BDC assets are financed with equity and about half on average are leveraged with a floating-rate facility. In short, only about a quarter of total assets for the average BDC is immune to changes in short-term rates, with the income of the other three-quarters directly linked to short-term rates.

Apart from Ares, a number of other BDCS are issuing bonds. The Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) is issuing $400 of 5.65% 2030 notes. Use of proceeds includes paying down the credit facility.

Great Elm Capital (GECC) is issuing $50 million of 7.75% 2030 notes. These will trade on the exchange under the ticker GECCG. Use of proceeds includes redeeming its 8.75% 2028 notes as well as a number of their other bonds, most of which carry higher coupons than GECCG.

There are a number of reasons why BDCs are issuing bonds. One, is that in some cases they have bonds coming due, and they want to keep the same amount of unsecured issuance.

Two, they want to rotate from credit facilities to bonds, perhaps as a way to decrease their floating-rate liabilities. Three, they want to increase their lending, and they view bonds as the best way to source additional financing.

And four, they may just want to lock in what they view as attractive borrowing rates. The high-yield corporate bond yield is trading near its lowest post-2022 level.

Overall, we are seeing all of these reasons in play. Bonds are coming due, lenders are rotating more into bonds from credit facilities to take advantage of tight public market spreads, and lenders are likely looking to add dry powder while market liquidity is strong.

Two of our positions OTF and FDUS have very low leverage which we expect to move higher over time and support net investment income. We also expect leverage to rise somewhat across the sector, which will partly offset the continued decline in short-term rates from further Fed rate cuts.

Market Commentary

Blue Owl Technology Finance (OTF) partly brought its December lock-up expiry earlier. Instead of a 32% December lock-up expiry, there will be a 10% lock-up expiry in September, with the rest still scheduled as before. The company did something similar with one of its other BDCs last year. It is likely looking to take advantage of high market liquidity and strong risk appetite rather than take its chances in December. Splitting up lock-up expiries should also lead to lower stock volatility, though dip buyers might be miffed by the new schedule.

We continue to maintain a position in the stock given its very attractive valuation and underlying outperformance.

data.title: Eventbrite: Limping Along, Savings Insufficient To Offset Top-Line Declines
data.description:
At one point, news headlines proclaimed that younger generations were reducing their spending on objects and clothes in order to prioritize spending on experiences, like travel and concert tickets. In the current macroeconomic climate, however, it seems that even spending on experiences is showing signs of cracking.

This is readily evident in data for Eventbrite (NYSE:EB), the online ticketing platform. The company has seen a substantial decline in paid ticket volumes, which is a reflection of weaker discretionary spending. Since the start of the year, shares of Eventbrite have shed ~20% of their value, although the stock has also rallied ~30% relative to year-to-date peaks around $2. The question now for investors is: can Eventbrite execute a full rebound, or is it a value trap?

I last wrote a bearish opinion on Eventbrite in June, when the stock was trading closer to $2.50. While I am directionally more positive on the company after seeing paid creator and MAU counts rise sequentially and rebound, we still haven’t seen a meaningful enough uptick in paid ticket volumes. With this in mind, I’m reiterating my sell opinion on Eventbrite.

Recall that last year, Eventbrite made the decision to stop charging event organizer fees, which cut off its marketplace revenue. In return, the company had been hoping that the overall growth of its platform from free creators and free event attendees would cast a halo around the entire business.

This hasn’t yet materialized. Though creators are up, average tickets sold per creator are receding, which is an indicator of a weakened macroeconomy. Meanwhile, Eventbrite has resorted to selling ad space, but even ~50% y/y growth in ad revenue is insufficient to offset ticketing declines, as it forms only a chunk of the company’s $7 million marketplace revenue, which in turn is less than 10% of the company’s overall revenue.

Where I will give Eventbrite credit is that the company has been aggressively managing down its costs. The chart below showcases the company’s run rate of quarterly opex, now down to just $55 million per quarter. Again, however, though expense reductions have helped in stabilizing adjusted EBITDA, the threat of further top-line decay blunts these efforts.

Eventbrite is a cheap stock: but given choppy performance, I view it more as a value trap. At current share prices just under $3, Eventbrite trades at a market cap of $261.5 million. Netting off the $524.5 million of cash and funds receivable on the company’s latest balance sheet against $322.0 million of funds owed to creators and $241.5 million of debt on the company’s latest balance sheet, Eventbrite’s resulting enterprise value is $300.5 million.

Meanwhile, as shown in the chart below, Eventbrite has reduced its full-year revenue outlook to $290-$296 million (versus $295-$310 million previously, which represents a ~2 point cut to growth expectations) while its cost-cut plans have given the company more confidence in boosting adjusted EBITDA margins (on a lower revenue profile) to 7%, implying $20.5 million in midpoint adjusted EBITDA.

This positions Eventbrite's valuation at 14.6x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. If the company can truly gain meaningful operating leverage on its adjusted EBITDA and boost margins to the teens, then the stock is cheap: but the path to get there requires better stabilization in demand, which we're not yet seeing at all. To me, Eventbrite is a value trap best avoided.

Q2 Download

Let's now go through Eventbrite's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Eventbrite's revenue declined -14% y/y to $72.8 million, which slightly beat Wall Street's expectations of $71.7 million (-15% y/y) by a one-point margin. The company's pace of decline was the same as a -14% decline in Q1.

The bright side for Eventbrite is the fact that the company's y/y decline in paid creators appears to be moderating, down -5% y/y in Q2 to 168k creators: two points better than a -7% decline in Q1, and representing a sequential add of 10k creators. The company notes that after the close of Q1, the company's y/y declines moderated even further to -1% y/y.

But this has also been accompanied by fewer tickets sold per paid creator, which to me is a telling signal of weak consumer demand and inability to spend on discretionary events. The company's count of paid tickets declined -7% y/y to 20 million: and as a reminder here, Eventbrite's cut from paid tickets represents roughly 90% of the company's overall revenue. We note that ticketing revenue itself declined -10% y/y, outpacing ticket volume declines of -7% and indicating lower revenue per ticket.

Management views the reduction in paid ticket volumes per creator as a short-term issue, but I view it as a symptom of the weak macroeconomy. Per CEO Julie Hartz's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

As a proof point of our stabilization, paid creators and paid ticket volume were very close to flat year-over-year in July. This is a powerful signal that our recovery is taking hold, and it reinforces our confidence that we're on a clear path to resume paid ticket growth as we exit the year. While paid transacting creator volume is improving in line with expectations, the average number of paid tickets sold per creator is lower than we anticipated.

We view this as a short-term mix issue. We're addressing this with product and go-to-market support to lift productivity and drive higher ticket volumes in the back half of the year."

And though we note it's not directly comparable because Eventbrite focuses on primary market ticket sales and StubHub has a large secondary market business, we note that StubHub's upcoming IPO is oversubscribed, and the rival ticketing platform generated 11% y/y growth in GMS (gross marketplace sales) in the first half of FY25, directly outperforming Eventbrite's double-digit declines.

Again, where we will give Eventbrite credit is that as it's facing demand declines, the company is aggressively reacting by continuing to shave down costs. But ultimately, the reduction in ticket volumes is decimating profitability: adjusted EBITDA of $6.4 million declined -50% y/y, hitting a 9% margin that suffered -6 points of y/y deleverage.

Key Takeaways

Eventbrite is in a tough position. While it's true that the tough macroeconomy is to blame, Eventbrite's direct peer StubHub is doing much better, achieving double-digit marketplace sales growth so far in 2025, so Eventbrite's brand execution is partially to blame too. Despite aggressive cost-cutting measures, the company's adjusted EBITDA is declining ~50%, with savings insufficient to offset revenue declines. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: IBIT: Institutional Demand Is A Catalyst For Price Appreciation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recently covered the iShares Bitcoin ETF (NEOE:IBIT:CA), the Canadian-listed version of iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (NASDAQ:IBIT). The TSX-listed IBIT:CA ETF, owns the U.S.-listed IBIT, as its singular investment. I continue to see IBIT and its Canadian vehicle, IBIT:CA, as an accessible and cost effective way for traditional investors to gain exposure to Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

The legitimization of BTC through institutional participation and constructive regulatory changes has buoyed strong demand for BTC. Given the scarcity of BTC supply, I see increasing institutional demand and global acceptance as potent catalysts for long-term price appreciation. IBIT and for Canadian investors, IBIT:CA, BlackRock’s trusted ETF structure, low fees and ample liquidity make this ETF a straightforward way for investors to participate in the bull case for BTC without having to navigate BTC custodial risks and cryptocurrency exchanges.

My approach to cryptocurrencies has been characterized by curiosity, skepticism and pragmatism. Although cryptocurrency's touted benefits as a medium of exchange and store of value continue to be questionable in my opinion, it has undoubtedly emerged as a viable alternative asset class. I see BTC in particular as the dominant medium in that asset class and the product with the most staying power and acceptance.

Scarcity

The current market capitalization of BTC has reached $2.27T, representing approximately 58% of the $3.88T cryptocurrency market. The 21 million BTC available create an inherent scarcity of available BTC driving long-term price pressure. Institutional ownership has added to scarcity by removing coins from liquid supply and holding them in custodial wallets. Spurred by corporate and institutional accumulation, approximately 70% of BTC is held in inactive wallets.

New daily BTC issuance is now averaging less than 450 coins, a significant decline from previous years. Following the 2024 halving event, in which the rewards for new coins mined decreased following the 210,000 coins in that block being mined, the incentives for miners to produce new coins decreased, further limiting new supply. According to Fidelity Digital Assets, as of June 2025, the “ancient supply” of BTC, defined as coins that have been inactive for longer than ten years, now exceeds new supply, with 566 BTC per day crossing the 10 year inactive threshold, while only 450 BTC are added in new supply.

Growing Adoption as a Catalyst

Regulatory clarity under the Trump Administration has been a driver of institutional adoption. Growing adoption among institutional investors has been preceded by corporate demand. Publicly traded companies own approximately 850,000 BTC, or 4% of the total supply. Corporate adoption and the launch of Spot Bitcoin ETFs has enhanced the liquidity of BTC making it more attractive and viable for institutional investors.

According to consultancy EY, 86% of institutional investors surveyed had exposure to digital assets or planned to make these allocations in 2025. Of the 352 institutional investors surveyed by EY, 59% planned to allocate 5% or more of AUM to cryptocurrencies in 2025, with U.S. institutions indicating higher allocations.

Beyond adoption by pension funds and institutional wealth management firms, BTC adoption has been touted by some governmental entities well, including the U.S. Government’s proposed Strategic Bitcoin Reserve and Mubadala, Abu Dhabi’s Sovereign Wealth Fund. As of March 31, 2025, UAE-based Mubadala owned 8,726,972 units of IBIT, up from 8,235,533 shares reported at the end of 2024. At current prices, this position would be valued at $579M. Although this remains a small share of the fund’s $330B in AUM, the significant position held by a sovereign wealth fund represents growing acceptance and confidence among the worlds' largest asset managers and wealth funds.

Institutional ownership of IBIT has continued to accelerate with overall, institutional ownership now standing at 28% of the ETF's NAV. IBIT has higher institution ownership than other Spot Bitcoin ETFs. Reuters reported that as of July, analysts estimate that:

Less than 5% of all spot bitcoin Exchange Traded Fund assets are held by long-term investors such as pension funds and endowments, with another 10% to 15% owned by hedge funds or wealth management firms.

Over the past 12 months, there were 1,250 institutional buyers of IBIT, compared to 301 institutional sellers. Institutional cash inflows were $10.15B over the year, nearly 10X the $1.27B cash outflows from institutional investors and hedge funds. Overall, institutions hold a total of 375,504,410 units of IBIT, worth approximately $24B. Some of the largest institutional unit holders include Brevan Howard Capital Management LP, Goldman Sachs Group Inc, Susquehanna International Group, Llp, Jane Street Group, Llc, Millennium Management Llc and Citadel Advisors Llc.

Internationalization

According to the 2025 Global State of Crypto Report, a publication by Gemini, nearly two in five crypto owners in the US are invested in crypto ETFs. While the U.S. has a cryptocurrency adoption rate of 22%, participation among many emerging economies lags. Globally, just 1.29% of the world’s population owns BTC, equating to an approximately 106 million people worldwide.

In India for example, cryptocurrency adoption is just 7%. According to Matador Technologies Inc. (MATA:CA) with a median population under 30 and rapid adoption of brokerage accounts, India presents a significant opportunity. The country currently has 107 million cryptocurrency users and 192 million brokerage accounts, growing by 3.4 million/month. This demographic profile of young, tech savvy investors coupled with rapid adoption of brokerage accounts indicates that India offers significant potential for growth in BTC utilization.

Risk Analysis

Despite the incredible price appreciation BTC has enjoyed over the last decade, its prominence as measured by market capitalization has continued to diminish. As of September 2025, the market cap of BTC is $2.22T, suggesting a BTC dominance of 56%. While still the dominant cryptocurrency, this share has declined from nearly 85% in September 2015. Over that time, Ethereum (ETH-USD) has grown to approximately 13% of the cryptocurrency universe market value. The market cap for stablecoins has reached $290B, representing a 7.3% share of the total crypto market cap. While no other digital asset has seen as broad adoption by institutional investors as BTC, it is not a certainty that this advantage will endure indefinitely.

Correlation to Other Assets

BTC is often presented as a hedge against equity market fluctuations. Comparisons are sometimes made to gold, suggesting that BTC is a defensive asset class. It’s true that in its earlier days BTC wasn’t highly correlated to equity performance. With the widespread adoption of BTC by corporations and institutional investors, BTC has become increasingly correlated to equity performance. According to Dr. Mark Shore of CME Group, between 2017-2019, BTC’s correlation to equities was near zero. From 2020 to 2022, however, the medians correlation range was above 0.4. Despite the diminishing role BTC might play as an uncorrelated asset, for most equity investors, adding BTC to their portfolio will add the diversification of a less correlated asset class.

Constructive Regulatory Environment

The most significant risk to growing adoption of BTC by institutional investors is the regulatory environment. In the United States, changes under the Trump Administration have created a constructive framework for the normalization and expansion of institutional ownership of cryptocurrency. The SEC’s approval of BTC ETFs as well as the recent CLARITY and GENIUS Acts have enabled institutional holders to own BTC in financial products for savings and retirement. According to McMillan:

Digital Asset Market Clarity Act of 2025(the “CLARITY Act”), a U.S. federal bill that establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework for the classification, offering, trading, and supervision of digital assets.

This act articulates jurisdictional considerations between the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission by defining digital assets and digital commodities. The GENIUS Act, which passed the U.S. Senate on June 17, 2025 with bi-partisan support, provides a regulatory framework for the issuance and payment of stable coins. The cumulative effect of this regulatory clarity is a compelling narrative of BTC’s continuing trajectory of maturity and legitimacy. The advancement of this regulation has been accompanied by an inflow into BTC ETFs.

Conclusion

I continue to see IBIT as a cost effective and convenient way for individual investors to gain exposure to BTC. Fees to own IBIT are 0.25%, while the Canadian-listed IBIT:CA has a slightly higher management fee of 0.32%. The ETF format allows investors to add BTC through their own brokerage accounts by using a familiar ETF provider, BlackRock.

The bull case for BTC boils down to inherent scarcity of supply driven by growing demand from broader acceptance. The growing adoption of BTC broadly and Spot Bitcoin ETFs by institutional investors signals legitimacy and functional acceptance of BTC as an asset class.

As institutional investors and corporations continue to add exposure to BTC, an increasing portion of liquid supply becomes unavailable for short term trading. The entrance of institutional investors into this asset class is a virtuous cycle, where increasing institutional participation signals acceptance and enhances the scarcity narrative by adding demand and removing tradable coins from circulation.

Comments on My Own BTC Exposure

My personal ownership of BTC remains a modest part of my overall portfolio, nonetheless, I see having some exposure as a prudent allocation to this emerging asset class. I continue to own BTC directly as my initial ownership position predates the launch of IBIT in January, 2024.
